title: Black soldier fly larvae in a compost bin
subtitle: What they are, whether to remove them, and how to manage the population
excerpt: Black soldier fly (Hermetia illucens) larvae appear in outdoor compost bins in warm months, often in very large numbers. They consume food waste rapidly and are harmless to humans. This is the guide to identifying them correctly, understanding whether their presence is beneficial or a sign of an imbalanced heap, and managing the population.
sourceNotes: Diener S et al, 'Fly larvae for organic waste treatment' (Eawag/Sandec, 2009). WRAP 'Managing pests in home composting' guidance. Garden Organic pest and beneficial insect identification guides.

glossaryTerms.definition: Hermetia illucens, a fly species whose larvae (BSFL) are highly efficient decomposers of food waste, consuming it at rates up to five times faster than traditional composting. They do not transmit disease and the adults do not bite or feed during their short lifespan.
The excrement and shed skins of insect larvae, particularly from composting larvae. BSFL frass is rich in nutrients and is a useful soil amendment.

content.text: Opening a hot outdoor bin in summer to find it seething with fat white or grey-cream larvae is alarming but not a problem.

larvae (BSFL) are among the most effective decomposers of food waste known. A population of BSFL can reduce a food-waste-heavy bin's contents by 50 to 80% in a week. They do not emerge as pest flies, do not bite, and the finished
they leave behind is a useful soil amendment.

BSFL are 15 to 25 mm long, grey-brown to cream in colour, with a flattened body and a small distinct head. They move slowly compared to blow-fly larvae (maggots), which are white, pointed at both ends, and writhe actively. BSFL do not typically inhabit meat or carrion; they prefer plant-based food waste and avoid protein sources that attract blow flies. If you see white pointed maggots in large numbers and the bin contains meat waste, those are blow flies. That is a different management problem.
When BSFL are a sign of imbalance

A very large BSFL population (the bin appears more larvae than compost) indicates the heap is receiving far more food waste than carbon material, and the decomposition has become wet and anaerobic. The bin will smell unpleasant in this state. BSFL thrive in oxygen-poor conditions; they are not causing the anaerobic problem but they indicate it. Add carbon materials (shredded cardboard, wood chip, torn newspaper) in a 3:1 ratio to food waste by volume, and mix thoroughly to re-establish aerobic conditions.

In areas where BSFL populations are reliable in summer (southern England, generally), some composters deliberately maintain them. The larvae self-harvest by climbing out of the bin when mature, collecting in the lower chamber or in a bucket under a ramp. They can be used as protein feed for chickens, fish, or reptiles. The remaining frass is collected as a nitrogen-rich soil amendment.

BSFL are active in the UK from June to September in most years. They do not survive UK winters outdoors; populations collapse naturally as temperatures drop below 15°C. The adult fly needs temperatures above 27°C to reproduce, so unmanaged populations do not persist year-round in the UK without artificial heating.

attrs.symptom: Bin contents are mostly larvae, not compost

content.text: Add four buckets of shredded cardboard or wood chip for every bucket of food waste added in the past two weeks. Turn the contents to incorporate and restore aerobic conditions.

attrs.symptom: Large white pointed maggots, not BSFL, with meat odour

content.text: Remove any meat or fish waste from the bin immediately. Bury the contents under 150 mm of carbon material. Consider fitting a lockable, pest-proof bin if meat waste is unavoidable.
